title: Independently-trained day/night specialist twins with a trunk-free brightness dispatcher
hypothesis: 20 straight reverted rounds have exhaustively swept decode/output parameterizations (dense field, per-patch voting + attention pooling, per-patch voting + IRLS robust consensus, VQ codebook, continuous GMM, coarse-to-fine, retrieve-then-rerank, spatial MoE, canonicalization translators) on top of trunks that were EITHER the banned pretrained MobileNetV3-Small OR a from-scratch conv stack -- and every single one lost to champion (exp-35, 743.07m). The three prior 'dispatcher' rounds in this history (exp-30, exp-46, exp-50) all share one thing none of the decode sweeps questioned: the dispatcher blends two or more expert HEADS that read off a SINGLE SHARED trunk/backbone, trained jointly end to end every step -- so gradients from night crops still shape the features a day crop's expert reads, and vice versa. That is soft task-blending inside one optimization, not specialization. This round's forced pivot bans the champion's shared trunk outright, which finally makes the untested version of the dispatcher idea cheap to build correctly: TWO fully independent from-scratch trunks, each trained in its OWN optimization run on only its own half of the lighting buckets, so a night crop's gradient never touches a single day-specialist weight and vice versa -- true task decomposition, not joint multi-task blending. If the champion's ~743m worst-case has been capped by one shared representation being pulled in two directions (day's rich-texture regime vs. night's near-featureless regime), removing that shared-gradient interference by training two small purpose-built specialists in isolation, then only calibrating a tiny trunk-free dispatcher on top, should relax that cap -- at the cost of each specialist getting a smaller capacity budget than champion's single trunk, since both must fit the same 4 MiB envelope together.
method: Replace the single shared-trunk model with two independent from-scratch specialist networks -- Day (morning/midday/afternoon) and Night (early_evening/evening/night) -- each its own small conv trunk + coarse classification field + sharpened soft-argmax decode + confidence head, no weights shared between them and no pretrained init anywhere (mobilenet_v3_small / mnv3s_features8.pt banned this round). A third, tiny, trunk-free dispatcher (3 raw-pixel brightness statistics -> 2-layer MLP -> sigmoid gate g) blends the two specialists' (u, v, conf) outputs for the final export. Training is staged, not joint: Phase 1 trains only the Day specialist on its 3 buckets; Phase 2 trains only the Night specialist on its 3 buckets, from a fresh optimizer with zero gradient crossover into Phase 1's weights; Phase 3 freezes both specialists completely and trains only the ~40-parameter gate across all 6 buckets on the frozen specialists' blended output. One ONNX graph is exported at the end (both specialists always run; the gate blend, not graph branching, does the routing), preserving the single-file single-forward-pass export contract.
expected_outcome: If shared-trunk cross-lighting gradient interference has been the real ceiling (not decode design, which 20 rounds already ruled out), worst-case median error should drop meaningfully below champion's 743.07 m, plausibly into the 400-600 m range, with night improving more than day (night gradients no longer diluted by day's easier signal). Risk: each specialist's capacity is roughly half of champion's single trunk to fit the shared 4 MiB budget, and the classification grid is coarser (~16x16 vs champion's 32x32) -- if capacity-per-regime turns out to matter more than gradient interference, this could underperform champion, which would itself be an informative result ruling out the interference hypothesis and pointing back at deployment-budget capacity as the real constraint.
eli5: Instead of one brain trying to memorize the city in both daylight and pitch darkness at once, we build two smaller, separate brains -- one that only ever studies daytime photos, one that only ever studies night photos -- trained completely apart so neither one's lessons get diluted by the other's. A tiny doorman glances at how bright the incoming photo is and decides which brain should answer. We're testing whether the old one-brain-does-everything setup was quietly making both jobs a little worse than they could be on their own.

stages.name: Camera frame
stages.detail: 128x128 px aerial crop, one of 6 simulated lighting conditions

stages.name: Dispatcher
stages.detail: reads only 3 cheap raw-pixel brightness statistics of the incoming crop (mean luminance, luminance spread, fraction of very dark pixels) -- no trunk, no learned features -- through a tiny 2-layer network into one sigmoid number: how day-like vs night-like does this crop look

stages.name: Day-specialist trunk
stages.detail: a brand-new, never-pretrained small conv stack, trained from scratch and ONLY ever shown morning/midday/afternoon crops -- never sees a single night-bucket gradient

stages.name: Day-specialist field + decode
stages.detail: the day trunk's features feed a coarse classification grid over the map plus a contrast-sharpened balance-point decode and a heat-map-shape confidence read -- champion's proven decode mechanism, rebuilt at a coarser resolution to fit two specialists in the same size budget

stages.name: Night-specialist trunk
stages.detail: an independent, separately-initialized copy of the same small conv stack shape as the day trunk, but with its own separately-trained weights, trained from scratch and ONLY ever shown early_evening/evening/night crops -- never sees a single day-bucket gradient, never shares a weight with the day trunk

stages.name: Night-specialist field + decode
stages.detail: same recipe as the day specialist's field + decode, own independent weights

stages.name: Blend
stages.detail: the dispatcher's day-vs-night number mixes the two specialists' independent (lat, lon, confidence) guesses into one; when the dispatcher itself is unsure which specialist to trust (a genuinely dusk-like crop), the blended confidence is nudged down on top of the two specialists' own confidence blend, since an uncertain hand-off is itself a reason to be less sure

stages.name: Training data
stages.detail: the existing confusability-weighted place sampler (exp-35) is kept as the sampling mechanism but now runs twice, independently: once building the day specialist's per-epoch draw from only its 3 buckets' look-alike measurements, once for the night specialist's 3 buckets -- the two draws never mix

stages.name: Training procedure
stages.detail: three separate stages instead of one joint run: (1) train the day trunk+field+decode+confidence to convergence on day buckets only, fresh optimizer; (2) train the night trunk+field+decode+confidence to convergence on night buckets only, a completely separate fresh optimizer, zero gradient ever crosses into stage 1's weights; (3) freeze every specialist weight from stages 1-2 and train only the ~40-parameter dispatcher gate across all 6 buckets on top of the frozen, already-final specialist outputs

stages.name: Training signal
stages.detail: stages 1-2 each use champion's proven per-specialist loss (Gaussian-smoothed cross-entropy against the specialist's own coarse field + expected-coordinate L2 + confidence BCE), computed completely independently per specialist; stage 3 minimizes an L2/Huber loss on the final BLENDED (u, v) against ground truth plus a confidence BCE on the blended confidence, with gradients flowing only into the gate's ~40 parameters

implementation_brief:
Rewrite model/model.py and model/train.py together; do not touch anything outside model/ (pipeline/*, autoresearch/schema.sql, areas.yaml etc. are frozen per /FROZEN). PRESERVE two hard contracts stated in model/model.py's module docstring: (1) train.py's CLI is unchanged -- same invocation shape the harness already calls; (2) export_onnx(model, path) must still produce a single ONNX graph taking a 1x3x128x128 float32 input in [0,1] named 'frame' and returning one output named 'uvc' shaped [[u, v, conf]] via torch.onnx.export(..., opset_version=17, dynamo=False) -- no control-flow ops, both specialists must always execute in one static forward pass (the gate blends their outputs; it must never branch the graph). Do NOT import torchvision.models.mobilenet_v3_small anywhere, do NOT reference model/pretrained/mnv3s_features8.pt or load any pretrained checkpoint at all -- every parameter in this design is freshly initialized.

model/model.py: replace the current single-trunk TinyLocNet with three small nn.Module pieces: (a) SpecialistNet(grid_k=16) -- a from-scratch 4-stage plain conv stack (channels roughly 32->64->96->128, stride 2 each stage, 128x128 input -> 8x8 feature grid, matching the size this codebase's earlier from-scratch experiments already used successfully), GAP descriptor + a 1x1-conv layout squeeze (keep layout_ch small, e.g. 6-8 channels, to control param count) concatenated into one descriptor, a Linear field head to grid_k*grid_k logits, the SAME sharpened-softmax soft-argmax decode math champion already uses (DECODE_BETA-scaled softmax over fixed cell centers -> expected u, v), and a small confidence head reading (GAP descriptor, sharpened-field peak mass, sharpened-field entropy, gap between sharpened and unsharpened decode) exactly like champion's conf_head, ending in sigmoid(z - conf_shift) with its own conf_shift buffer. forward(x) takes a raw 128x128 crop (no C4 rotation fan this round -- rely on random-rotation training augmentation instead, freeing the compute budget the rotation fan used to spend, since two specialist passes already cost roughly what champion's single 4-way rotation pass cost) and returns (u, v, conf, logits) so the training loss can read logits directly. (b) Dispatcher -- no conv trunk at all: from x compute mean luminance (x.mean over channel+spatial), luminance std, and dark-pixel fraction (fraction of pixels with per-pixel mean below a fixed constant like 0.15), concatenate those 3 scalars, feed a 2-layer MLP (hidden ~8) to one sigmoid gate g. (c) DispatcherLocNet -- holds self.day = SpecialistNet(...), self.night = SpecialistNet(...), self.gate = Dispatcher(); forward(x) always runs both specialists and the gate, then blends: u = g*u_day + (1-g)*u_night, v likewise, conf = g*conf_day + (1-g)*conf_night - 0.15 * binary_entropy(g), clamped to [0,1]; returns torch.cat([u,v,conf], dim=1) as the sole export output (this is the object export_onnx() receives). Keep a way for train.py to construct a bare SpecialistNet standalone (for phases 1-2, so only that specialist's parameters exist in the optimizer) and to assemble the final DispatcherLocNet from two trained SpecialistNet state dicts for phase 3 / export. Define specialist_loss_fn(pred, logits, target_uv, grid_k) mirroring champion's existing loss_fn (Gaussian-smoothed CE against the specialist's own grid_k field + expected-coord L2 + 0.3*conf BCE against a GOOD_ERR_UV hit label) for phases 1-2, and a separate gate_loss_fn(blended_pred, target_uv) (Huber or L2 on (u,v) + 0.3*conf BCE) for phase 3. Size check: after wiring this up, export and check the .onnx file size directly -- target combined size comfortably under the 4 MiB gate (pipeline/score.py MODEL_MAX_BYTES), e.g. aim for <=3.5 MiB to leave margin; if over, first shrink grid_k (try 12 instead of 16) before shrinking trunk channel widths, since the field head's Linear grows quadratically with grid_k while the trunk is roughly fixed cost.

model/train.py: restructure the per-area training entry point into three sequential phases sharing the existing data-loading/sampling machinery (LIGHTING_BUCKETS from pipeline/common.py; DAY_BUCKETS = ['morning','midday','afternoon'], NIGHT_BUCKETS = ['early_evening','evening','night']). Phase 1: build a SpecialistNet, a fresh optimizer, run the existing confusability-weighted per-epoch resampling training loop (per exp-17/20/25's kept mechanics) restricted to DAY_BUCKETS only, using specialist_loss_fn, then run the existing conf-calibration step (per exp-15's mechanics: shift conf_shift so >=40% coverage per bucket on train-split crops) restricted to DAY_BUCKETS. Phase 2: identical procedure, fresh SpecialistNet, fresh optimizer, restricted to NIGHT_BUCKETS, completely independent from phase 1 -- do not reuse or warm-start from the day specialist's weights or optimizer state in any way. Phase 3: instantiate DispatcherLocNet from the two trained specialists, set requires_grad=False on every day.* and night.* parameter (only gate.* parameters should appear in the phase-3 optimizer), run a short training loop (a handful of epochs is enough -- the gate has ~40 parameters) sampling across ALL 6 buckets using gate_loss_fn on the blended output, then run one more conf-calibration pass on the FINAL blended conf output across all 6 buckets so the exported conf_shift keeps coverage >= 40% per bucket. Keep the total step/epoch budget for phases 1+2 combined in the same ballpark as champion's single-run budget (split roughly in half per specialist, since each only has 3 buckets' worth of data instead of 6) so overall wall-clock per area stays comparable to prior rounds; phase 3 is cheap and short. End by calling export_onnx(assembled_model, out_path) exactly as before -- one ONNX file per area, same path convention the harness already expects.
